title: Making a test skein before weaving
subtitle: Why the sett calculation must use the post-wet-finishing yarn width, not the fresh-off-bobbin measurement
excerpt: A sett calculation depends on how wide the yarn actually is after washing and drying, not how thick it looks coming off the bobbin. Handspun yarn can change significantly during wet-finishing: it may bloom and widen, or it may tighten and narrow. A small 50-wrap test skein, washed and dried before you measure WPI, takes less than an afternoon and saves a full project from incorrect sett.
sourceNotes: The test skein method for establishing post-wet-finishing WPI before sett calculation is standard practice in handweaving and taught in introductory weaving and spinning courses. The relationship between WPI and sett is documented across weaving reference texts including Peggy Osterkamp and Ashenhurst's rule.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wraps per inch: the number of times a yarn wraps around a ruler or gauge in one inch (2.5 cm) without stretching or gaps. Used to classify yarn weight and to calculate weaving sett. Always measure WPI on the finished, wet-finished yarn: the pre-wash measurement can be misleading.

The number of warp ends per inch (EPI) in a woven cloth. Calculated from the yarn's WPI: a standard balanced sett is approximately half the WPI for a plain weave. A sett that is too close produces a warp-faced cloth; too open and the warp yarns slip apart. The correct sett depends on the finished yarn width, not the yarn as spun.

The process of washing, drying, and sometimes pressing a handspun skein or woven cloth to set the twist, bloom the fibre, and stabilise the structure. Wet-finishing changes the yarn dimensions: high-twist singles narrows slightly; woollen-spun yarn with natural oils blooms outward. The post-wet-finishing measurement is the one that represents how the yarn will behave in a finished cloth.

content.text: Weaving sett is the number of warp ends you thread per inch across the loom. If the sett is wrong for the yarn, the cloth will be too dense to beat or too open to hold together. The sett is calculated from the yarn's
WPI
: roughly half the WPI gives you a balanced plain-weave
sett
. The calculation is straightforward, but it only works if the WPI is measured from yarn that has been through
wet-finishing
. Fresh-off-bobbin handspun yarn has active twist, and that active twist affects the apparent diameter. Measuring before washing gives a misleadingly precise number.
A 50-wrap test skein takes about 20 minutes to wind and wash. Compared to the time invested in warping a full loom, it is a negligible step.
What you need

items.name: Handspun yarn from the main spinning batch
items.qty: enough for 50 wraps on a niddy-noddy or around a book

items.name: Soft tape measure or ruler with inch markings
Small bowl of warm water for the wet-finishing soak

content.text: Step 1: Wind the test skein

Wind 50 wraps of your handspun yarn around a niddy-noddy, a book of known circumference, or any smooth, rigid form with a consistent size. Count wraps carefully. Tie the skein in two places with a contrasting yarn in a loose figure-of-eight knot: the tie must be loose enough not to compress the wraps.

Weigh the test skein on kitchen scales and record the weight. This step is optional for the sett calculation, but useful: if you know the weight of 50 wraps, you can calculate the approximate metreage per 100 g for the full batch.
Step 2: Wet-finish the test skein

Soak the skein in warm water with a small drop of washing-up liquid for 15-20 minutes. This is the same process you will use on the full skein after spinning. Use the same water temperature you plan to use for the project: the finishing temperature matters, because very warm water blooms a woollen-spun yarn more than cool water does.
Press the skein in a towel without wringing. Hang to dry at room temperature. Do not use a tumble dryer: the test must replicate the conditions the yarn will experience in use.
Step 3: Measure WPI after drying

Once fully dry, remove the test skein from its drying location and wrap the yarn around a ruler over a 2.5 cm (one inch) section. Wrap without stretching the yarn and without leaving gaps between the wraps. Count the number of wraps: this is the WPI.
Measure at three different points along the skein and average the results. Handspun yarn is not perfectly consistent in diameter, so some variation is normal.
Step 4: Calculate the sett

For a balanced plain weave, divide the WPI by two. A yarn that wraps at 12 WPI gets a sett of approximately 6 ends per inch. For a twill weave, which interlaces less frequently than plain weave, use two-thirds of the WPI: 12 WPI gives approximately 8 EPI for twill.

These are starting calculations, not fixed rules. Some yarn structures (very hairy, or with high loft) need a more open sett than the formula suggests; very smooth, dense yarns can go slightly closer. Wind a short sample warp at the calculated sett and weave 10 cm before committing to the full project warp.
Why fresh-off-bobbin measurement is unreliable

Freshly spun yarn holds active twist: each strand still wants to kink back on itself. The active twist makes the singles appear thinner than it will be after finishing, because the twist compresses the diameter. After wet-finishing and drying, a woollen-spun singles may bloom by 10-20% in diameter. A fine worsted-spun singles may narrow slightly as the twist beds in. Either way, the post-finishing measurement is what the cloth will actually see.
Common problems

items.problem: The WPI varies a lot across the test skein
items.cause: The handspun yarn is uneven in diameter, which is normal for handspun. The WPI count differs noticeably between thick and thin sections.
items.fix: Measure at five or more points and calculate an average. If the variation is very large (more than 3 WPI between thick and thin sections), consider plying the singles before weaving: plying averages out diameter variation significantly.

items.problem: The test skein looks noticeably different from the rest of the batch after finishing
items.cause: The finishing conditions (water temperature, drying method) were different for the test skein.
items.fix: Use exactly the same finishing process on the test skein as you will use for the full batch. If you use warm water with detergent for the batch, use the same for the test. Small differences compound: a cooler soak leaves more active twist, which changes the WPI.
